That is Mandalor status. Any questions? Again, I can go through tickets, but I think this is the most critical view of how the system is and how we are relative to the goal of getting to production.

As a state thing... So she... I'm going to show a code as much as we would to... So Ray, this comes down to that the stuff we talked about the specific... Not cancellation. But for whatever reason, we don't have something, and we know it upfront.
It's these specific product statuses. So, this is the proposed change, and what this is effectively doing is creating a new status that Thrive would read, which just says "canceled" or "scheduled," and then it would default to whatever the actual one is.
It's consolidating these different statuses like "not applicable," "abandoned," "change of mind," "health-related," "sent back to us," and that kind of thing. So, I think my question is, do we want to pipe through exactly what we get at CSTAR and then deal with it granularly as we need to downstream? Or do we want the polling service to actually make a decision about what it's going to show, Thrive? Personally, I'm for piping stuff straight through because I don't like pieces that are supposed to be throwaway interfaces getting smart.
But I don't know what the future looks like for this kind of ingestion around specific products.

content.speaker: Stace
content.text: It's a really good question. And both sides would throw away to tell you the truth. Yeah, so I guess it might be a little simpler just to let's see... Bunch dump a bunch of stuff in and filter on the thrive side because the article is cleaner and a little bit easier, more nimble to deal with.

content.speaker: Sam Hatoum
content.text: S points filtering.

content.speaker: Stace
content.text: On our side, it does reduce noise over the wire.

content.speaker: Sam Hatoum
content.text: Yeah, I think the single responsibility principle. The more facts we have on our side, the more power we have to deal with problems.

content.speaker: harry.dennen@llsa.com
content.text: Yeah, that's where I was at. Plus, if Ray comes to me tomorrow and says, "Hey, actually, I want this. I don't have to go change the polling service because Ray does that all the time."

content.speaker: Sam Hatoum
content.text:
I mean, you know, if you just change it from polling service to message mirroring service, for example, right? That's really what it is, right? And if it's message mirroring, you should do one thing and that is just mirroring the messages over.
So yeah.

content.speaker: harry.dennen@llsa.com
content.text: Well, it already does more than that because there's a few... There are all kinds of weird stuff we have to deal with, but not here. You're absolutely right. It should be that.

content.speaker: Sam Hatoum
content.text: If one of my tires is flat on the car, I certainly wouldn't go slash the other three.

content.speaker: harry.dennen@llsa.com
content.text: Yeah, no, I hear you. That's kind of where I'm at. I'm like rather just give me the raw stuff, you know, even if it's on like a raw property, and then I'll interpret it where I need to actually.

content.speaker: Stace
content.text:
Now that I'm even thinking about it from performance bottlenecks because we're faking events on the C-star side in a transactional system, the more filtering and logic we add is more overhead.
That's actually going to slow the entire monolith down. Because. Because we've killed the database already. This before. So we might as well just let it dump into it because it's que processing on the AWS side, right?
It's just lambless. It's magic. We can they take a little longer, they take a little longer, we throw more concurrency at it.

content.speaker: harry.dennen@llsa.com
content.text: Okay, so I think... It sounds like we're leaning towards just piping it, don't get smart at polling.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Yeah, the less smart we are at polling, the more information and the more flexible we are going forward.

content.speaker: Stace
content.text: Yeah, I respect once... I've been mentioned before to the group, but I'd like to see the system just work end to end, get orders, insert it in...

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: To... Re...

content.speaker: Stace
content.text:
We still have... We're going to have two PA goods we have to sort out the problem before we can really scale right because we're going to want the same identifier on the order and in the data stored to thrive as...
So I was thinking of the resulting entity. If we just launched this plain vanilla right now, they're going to have an identifier for the participant in the order and then the C-star identifier for the results.
Yeah. We got to chew that up at some point.

content.speaker: harry.dennen@llsa.com
content.text: I've been thinking about that. So in my previous life, we had some concepts of central authorities. We used it for a security thing where APIs couldn't just talk to each other. They had to check in with a central authority that had a registry of who was allowed to talk to who, but that central authority is the issuer of unique identifiers.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: [Laughter].

content.speaker: harry.dennen@llsa.com
content.text: So because we're doing this kind of strangler pattern, it almost makes sense to pull PGO creation out of CSTAR. So if it's just happening on like a procedure auto in.

content.speaker: Stace
content.text:
That is the right thing to do. But I don't know if we can do that per se until the day we get call center out of placing orders. C start right because still we turn on recurrently. We're going to start migrating traffic.
But the lion's share of the participants are still going to be graded. So it's...

content.speaker: harry.dennen@llsa.com
content.text: Gonna come to the... Yeah, I mean...

content.speaker: Stace
content.text:
I kind of think there's a way... And this was harder when you added more relational database or an orrcal world where we're around sequences and things like that. But in a good world, anything can create it. 100%.
So I'm wondering if there's a way once we get the order, the happy path orders just flowing into CSTAR can we... I don't even know if there are constraints, right? Maybe if we insert them with the good created by recurly, we can just stop it from creating its own PD or there might be a little code change to maybe do that.

content.speaker: harry.dennen@llsa.com
content.text: But that seems reasonable. Yeah.

content.speaker: Stace
content.text: For economics to think through ideas team. But, you know, I'm kind of waiting to see it.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Probably see how grow around.

content.speaker: Stace
content.text:
I don't want to throw a wrench in it before everything's working and we can start on all the tests. But then that might be one of the last cleanup items that we have to stitch in because that's going to ultimately be important for BI, right?
Because they're going to have to sort out the new data and thrive from the historic data. That's in Azure Data Lake and those identifiers have to stay consistent across people.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: [Laughter].

content.speaker: harry.dennen@llsa.com
content.text: I mean, there's always the option of version two, right? And we just have this idea of two different... I mean, it's not great, but V2 is...

content.speaker: Stace
content.text: It's not unheard of.

content.speaker: harry.dennen@llsa.com
content.text: Usually, with APIs, it generally works like that, right? When we have to change some sort of identification, then you just add a new property and call it V2 identifier and then you take a year to phase out the old one.

content.speaker: Stace
content.text: Okay, but overall, good progress. I think if we can get these last couple of items out of the door, make sure the pipeline is working, and make sure the sandbox is solid so we can begin the end testing...

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Of work everything.

content.speaker: bethany.duffy@llsa.com
content.text: Okay?

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Is there anything else? Are there any DES that are happening in the product office hours?

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Wesley, I think you want to share some additional UI tweaks that you've made yet.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Okay. So we've got some from Recurly.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Sam.com.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Then, are there any tickets that we have to refine in the product office?

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Want...

content.speaker: bethany.duffy@llsa.com
content.text: Krisp, I do have some new ones that I read from the team, but it sounds like they're pretty full for this week, so we can wait until they're more ready to pick it up so we're not doing it too far ahead.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: People may speak soon.

content.speaker: bethany.duffy@llsa.com
content.text: Okay, they're all production-like launch stories anyway. They're just the follow-up, so I don't need them going into the production launches.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text:
Okay. And then, just for anybody that wasn't in the end or stand-up when Willie started, today he's the new senior developer. I might have him join Harry instead of him joining the end stand-up. Tomorrow I might join Mandalor just to introduce him.
And then hop over to the end or tomorrow.

content.speaker: harry.dennen@llsa.com
content.text: Sounds good.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Talk to you all soon.

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: Sounds good's.

content.speaker: Jennifer
content.text: Five minutes.
